hook: A restorer chasing a leak found the buried colonial city under his own house
facts: Owner Jorge Eckstein began restoring the 1830 house in the 1980s and broke through to 18th-century foundations beneath
Excavations exposed brick tunnels that once channelled the Zanjon de Granados creek toward the river
Recovered remains include cisterns, a water tank, African pipes, English china and French tiles
Now toured as a private archaeology museum on Defensa 755
w: Hunting a damp problem in his 1830 townhouse in the 1980s, the owner instead punched through to the buried foundations of an 18th-century city, plus the brick tunnels that once piped a creek to the river. Cisterns, African pipes, French tiles, all dug from his own basement. Archaeology by accident.

n: Tunnels of the Manzana de las Luces
hook: Jesuit tunnels under the colonial block that nobody can fully explain
facts: The block held the Jesuit church of San Ignacio, the first national library, college and the early legislature
Brick tunnels likely begun in the late 1600s run beneath, with almost no construction records
Archaeologist Daniel Schavelzon argues they were an unfinished scheme to link the city's churches
Sections re-opened to guided visits from the entrance on Peru 272
legend: Theories cast them variously as escape routes, contraband smuggling runs, or Inquisition chambers
w: Under the colonial block that housed the Jesuits, the first national library and the early legislature runs a brick warren of tunnels begun around the 1690s, with almost no record of who dug them or why. One scholar calls it an abandoned plan to link the churches underground. They say smugglers and troops used them. Nobody is sure.

n: Museo del Agua y de la Historia Sanitaria
hook: A museum of antique toilets inside a wildly ornate French water palace
facts: Housed upstairs in the 1894 Palacio de Aguas Corrientes, a pump station disguised as a Beaux-Arts palace
The facade carries some 300,000 glazed terracotta tiles imported from Britain
Built to supply clean water after the 1871 yellow fever epidemic killed thousands
Free collection of period bidets, faucets, pipes and toilets at Riobamba 750
w: The city dressed a humble pumping station in 300,000 imported British tiles, building a Beaux-Arts palace to hold water tanks after yellow fever gutted the population in 1871. Upstairs sits the reward: a free, gloriously odd collection of antique bidets, faucets and toilets. Plumbing treated as fine art.

hook: Home of the painter who invented two languages and a mystical chess
facts: Dedicated to Alejandro Xul Solar (1887-1963), friend of Borges and a Latin American vanguard figure
He devised Pan-Lengua and Neocriollo, invented languages, plus a duodecimal number system
Built a modified piano and a spiritual 'pan-chess' on display in his former home
Sits in his old apartment block at Laprida 1212, run by the Fundacion Pan Klub
w: Borges's friend Xul Solar wanted to reform the universe, so he invented two languages, a duodecimal counting system, a re-tuned piano and a mystical chess. His old Palermo apartment now holds the lot, masks and astrological canvases included. A private cosmos, kept exactly where its maker left it.

hook: A grieving family built a church around the city's most famous ghost story
facts: Raised by the Guerrero family where their daughter Felicitas was shot dead by a rejected suitor in 1872
The only church in the city to hold secular statues, marble figures of Felicitas and her family
Consecrated in 1876, built on the grounds of the family's Barracas mansion
Stands on Isabel la Catolica 520 beside Plaza Colombia
legend: Every 30 January a weeping woman in white is said to appear, and handkerchiefs tied to the gates supposedly turn up wet with her tears
w: After a spurned suitor shot 26-year-old Felicitas Guerrero in 1872, her parents built this church on the family grounds and filled it with the only secular marble statues in any city church, her own likeness among them. Legend says she returns each 30 January in white, and handkerchiefs left at the gate come back wet with tears.

hook: One artist spent decades turning his street into a mosaic gallery
facts: Artist Marino Santa Maria began with his own facade at Lanin 33 in 1990
Over three blocks he painted and tiled some 40 house fronts in Venetian mosaic
The remade passage was unveiled with a street party on 19 April 2001
Runs between the railway tracks from Brandsen to Avenida Suarez
w: In 1990 the painter Marino Santa Maria tiled the front of his own house on a quiet Barracas lane, then kept going. Three blocks and roughly 40 facades later, his neighbours live behind swirling Venetian mosaic and colour. A whole street rebuilt as a single man's open-air canvas, one doorway at a time.

hook: A century-old cafe with the city's best billiard hall hidden in the basement
facts: Opened in 1894 as Avenida de Mayo was being cut through the city
The basement holds restored billiard tables more than 120 years old
Federico Garcia Lorca, lodging at the nearby Hotel Castelar, was a regular
Protected as a Bar Notable at Avenida de Mayo 1265
w: Open since 1894, this Avenida de Mayo cafe keeps its real life downstairs: a basement of restored billiard tables, some over 120 years old, where the city's sharpest players still gather. Lorca drank here while lodging up the road. Coffee at street level, green baize and cigarette smoke below.

hook: A skyscraper built as a stone model of Dante's Divine Comedy
facts: Architect Mario Palanti laid it out as hell, purgatory and heaven across 22 floors
The 100-metre height nods to the 100 cantos of the poem; nine arches mark hell's circles
A rooftop lighthouse was meant to beam to its twin, the Palacio Salvo, across the river in Montevideo
Inaugurated in 1923 at Avenida de Mayo 1370, once South America's tallest building
legend: Barolo is said to have planned to house Dante's ashes here, away from a collapsing Europe; they never left Ravenna
w: Mario Palanti built an entire skyscraper as Dante's poem: 22 floors rising from hell through purgatory to a heaven crowned by a lighthouse, 100 metres for the 100 cantos. The beam was meant to signal its twin tower across the river in Montevideo. They say Barolo even hoped to keep Dante's ashes up top.

hook: An Art Nouveau cake palace that sat shuttered and rotting for 20 years
facts: Designed by Francisco Gianotti, it opened on 9 July 1916 facing Congress
Named for an old flour mill, it wears a windmill and stained glass on a domed corner tower
Closed in 1997 and declared a National Historic Monument the same year
Congress expropriated it in 2014; it reopened for tours at Rivadavia 1815
w: Across from Congress stands an Art Nouveau confectionery crowned by a windmill, opened in 1916 and then left to rot for two decades after closing in 1997, its stained glass dimming behind grime. Congress finally seized it for restoration; the cake palace reopened for tours. Sugar-era splendour, dragged back from the dead.

hook: The arcade where Saint-Exupery wrote Night Flight, with a forgotten rooftop view
facts: Opened in 1915 and reckoned the city's first skyscraper, linking Florida and San Martin streets
Saint-Exupery lived on the sixth floor and wrote 'Night Flight' here
A long-shut 16th-floor mirador reopened to the public in 2010 with 360-degree views
An ornate Art Nouveau passage of marble, bronze and stained glass
w: The city's first skyscraper, this 1915 Art Nouveau arcade of bronze and stained glass hid a pilot-writer: Saint-Exupery lodged on the sixth floor and wrote 'Night Flight' above the passage. Up top, a mirador shut for decades reopened in 2010, giving a rooftop sweep most porteños forget exists.

hook: An aristocrat's mansion that became a 20-family tenement, now an antiques warren
facts: Built around 1880 for the Ezeiza family, who fled south after the 1871 yellow fever epidemic
Later packed as a conventillo where more than 20 immigrant families shared its patios
Three architects reworked it in 1980, keeping the original layout
Now a labyrinth of antique stalls at Defensa 1179
w: Built around 1880 for a wealthy family who soon fled the yellow fever wards moving south, this San Telmo mansion came down in the world: by the 1930s more than 20 immigrant families crammed its rooms, sharing one patio and kitchen. Today antique dealers haunt the same long passages. Grandeur, poverty, then bric-a-brac.

hook: A bronze tango idol whose fans keep a lit cigarette burning in his hand
facts: Tango singer Carlos Gardel died in a 1935 plane crash in Medellin, Colombia
The Art Deco mausoleum, finished in 1937, carries a life-size bronze of him by Manuel de Llano
Devotees slip a lit cigarette between the statue's fingers, busiest near his birthday
Among tango pantheons in the vast Chacarita cemetery
legend: Fans treat the figure less as a memorial than a presence still receiving visitors
w: Tango's greatest voice died in a 1935 air crash, and his fans never quite let him go. The Art Deco tomb in Chacarita carries a life-size bronze Gardel, and devotees tuck a lit cigarette between his fingers, busiest each birthday. They treat the statue less as a grave than as a man still taking callers.
